name: La Serena: Quintana, Zalamea and Esparragosa de la Serena — circular from Castuera
name_original: La Serena: Quintana, Zalamea  y Esparragosa de la Serena — circular desde Castuera
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Castuera traverse the expansive natural landscapes of Extremadura, characterized by vast dehesas and flatlands. The region offers diverse terrain, including long, flat stretches ideal for endurance cycling, alongside routes featuring gentle ascents. Cyclists can explore areas around the significant La Serena Reservoir and encounter varied landscapes with both open plains and elevated points offering panoramic views. This blend of natural beauty and cultural heritage defines the cycling experience in Castuera. Best road cycling routes around Castuera The most popular road cycling route is La Serena: Quintana, Zalamea and Esparragosa de la Serena — circular from Castuera, a 31.9 miles (51.3 km) trail that takes 2 hours 28 minutes to complete. This route features extensive flat sections through dehesas and natural areas. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Orellana la Vieja and Castillo de Puebla de Alcocer Loop, a difficult 74.8 miles (120.4 km) path. This route offers scenic views around the La Serena Reservoir and passes by historical castles. Local road cyclists also love the Cancho Roano – Quintana de la Serena loop from Centro de Interpretación de Cancho Roano, a 25.1 miles (40.5 km) trail leading through varied terrain and cultural sites, often completed in about 1 hour 55 minutes. Road cycling around Castuera is defined by expansive dehesas, the scenic La Serena Reservoir, and routes that integrate historical sites. The network offers options for moderate and difficult ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 30 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Castuera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Castuera?
A: Road cycling routes around Castuera traverse the expansive natural landscapes of Extremadura. You'll find a mix of vast &lt;strong&gt;dehesas&lt;/strong&gt; (Mediterranean woodlands) and flatlands, offering long, tranquil stretches ideal for endurance cycling. Some routes also feature gentle ascents and elevated points, providing panoramic views of the region.

Q: Are there routes suitable for advanced road cyclists seeking a challenge?
A: Yes, for experienced road cyclists, the region offers more challenging routes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992992123/orellana-la-vieja-and-castillo-de-puebla-de-alcocer-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orellana la Vieja and Castillo de Puebla de Alcocer Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is a difficult 74.8-mile (120.4 km) path with significant elevation changes, offering scenic views around the La Serena Reservoir and passing by historical castles.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in Castuera?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in the area are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993806019/la-serena-quintana-zalamea-and-esparragosa-de-la-serena-circular-from-castuera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Serena: Quintana, Zalamea and Esparragosa de la Serena — circular from Castuera&lt;/a&gt;, a 31.9-mile (51.3 km) trail featuring extensive flat sections through dehesas.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or natural attractions along the road cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You can enjoy scenic views around the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5422272" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Serena Dam&lt;/a&gt;, one of Europe's largest, or explore routes that offer panoramic vistas from elevated points. The expansive dehesas themselves provide picturesque landscapes throughout your ride.

Q: Are there any historical sites or landmarks I can visit while road cycling?
A: Yes, Castuera's road cycling routes can be combined with visits to significant historical and cultural sites. You might pass by ancient fortifications like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6085543" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Benquerencia de la Serena&lt;/a&gt;, or historical sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6088024" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Columns of Zalamea de la Serena&lt;/a&gt;, adding a cultural dimension to your ride.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Castuera?
A: Extremadura's climate makes spring and autumn ideal for road cycling, offering pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather. These seasons allow you to comfortably enjoy the expansive dehesas and scenic routes without the intense heat of summer or the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter.

Q: How long do road cycling routes typically take to complete in this area?
A: The duration varies depending on the route's length and your pace. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993806019/la-serena-quintana-zalamea-and-esparragosa-de-la-serena-circular-from-castuera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Serena: Quintana, Zalamea and Esparragosa de la Serena — circular from Castuera&lt;/a&gt; route, at 31.9 miles (51.3 km), typically takes about 2 hours 28 minutes. Longer routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992992123/orellana-la-vieja-and-castillo-de-puebla-de-alcocer-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orellana la Vieja and Castillo de Puebla de Alcocer Loop&lt;/a&gt; (74.8 miles), can take over 5 hours.

Q: Are there any moderate difficulty road cycling routes for intermediate riders?
A: Yes, the majority of road cycling routes around Castuera are rated as moderate, making them perfect for intermediate riders. An excellent option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40007914" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza de España loop from Zalamea de la Serena&lt;/a&gt;, a 34.5-mile (55.5 km) route that offers a balanced challenge through varied terrain.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Castuera?
A: The road cycling routes around Castuera are appreciated by the komoot community for their diverse terrain and scenic beauty. Over 30 road cyclists have used komoot to explore the area, often praising the extensive dehesas, the views around La Serena Reservoir, and the integration of historical sites into the routes.

Q: Are there options for longer road cycling tours in the Castuera region?
A: Certainly. For those looking for longer rides, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20129300" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villanueva de la Serena – La Haba loop from Villanueva de la Serena&lt;/a&gt; cover a substantial distance of 56.1 miles (90.2 km), offering an extended experience through the Extremaduran landscape.

Q: Where can I find starting points or trailheads for road cycling routes in Castuera?
A: Many routes conveniently start from towns or significant landmarks within the region. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38273327" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cancho Roano – Quintana de la Serena loop&lt;/a&gt; begins from the Centro de Interpretación de Cancho Roano, providing clear access to the trails.
tips: Quintana de La Serena is a small regional town in the La Serena region. The most important monument is the pre-Roman Hijovejo with its new cultural education center. Stop by the city church on the Spanish Steps to eat and drink during your trip and admire the heritage of the city center.

Cancho Roano is an archaeological site located in the municipality of Zalamea de la Serena. It is located three kilometers from Zalamea in the direction of Quintana de la Serena, in a small valley next to the Cagancha stream. One of the areas in the south of the peninsula with the largest deposits.

Zalamea de la Serena is a town full of history. With evidence of pre-Roman settlements found near the town, Roman and medieval monuments. Excellent starting point for outdoor activities towards the Zalamea Reservoir, La Charca, as it is known locally.

waypoints.name: Roman Columns of Zalamea de la Serena
